architecture.summary: The paper proposes that in-context learning (ICL) in transformer LLMs can be decomposed into two components: a learning algorithm ℒ that compresses a training set S into a query-agnostic task vector θ(S), and a rule-application function ℛ that applies θ to a query x to produce output, without direct dependence on S. The mechanism extracts θ from intermediate hidden states (at a learned layer ℓ*) using a dummy query, then patches θ back into a forward pass of the transformer run only on x, preventing ℛ from directly accessing S while maintaining ICL accuracy.

claims.statement: ICL can be viewed as compressing a training set S into a single task vector θ(S) calculated from S, which then modulates the transformer to produce output

claims.dataset: 18 tasks across algorithmic, translation, linguistic, and knowledge categories
claims.statement: The hypothesis-based procedure (extracting θ and applying ℛ with patching) maintains around 80-90% of the accuracy of regular ICL

claims.dataset: 18 tasks across algorithmic, translation, linguistic, and knowledge categories
claims.statement: The baseline (forward pass on query x alone, without demonstrations or task vector) reaches only 10-20% accuracy

claims.dataset: LLaMA 7B, 50 task vectors per task with varied S and dummy queries
claims.statement: Task vectors form distinct clusters in embedding space, with vectors from the same task closer together than vectors from different tasks, indicating stability of θ across variations in S and dummy query

claims.dataset: Conflicting task pairs (e.g. Next Letter vs To Upper, List Last vs List First)
claims.statement: When conflicting task vectors are injected during forward pass, the model achieves high accuracy on the injected task while largely disregarding the original demonstrations, showing that ℛ mainly relies on θ rather than directly accessing S

claims.dataset: vocabulary projection on LLaMA 13B and other models across all 18 tasks
claims.statement: Task vectors project onto interpretable tokens that semantically describe the task (e.g. 'English', 'translate' for French-to-English translation) without these terms appearing explicitly in the context
The intermediate layer ℓ* where θ is extracted exhibits a consistent performance peak across models of different sizes and layer counts, despite architectural differences

The paper studies relatively simple tasks with single-token outputs; it remains unclear whether the task vector mechanism generalizes to more complex ICL cases (e.g. arithmetic reasoning, multi-token outputs) that may require more elaborate parameterization
